specification.topic-statement: The OpenAI response parser extracts model, usage, finish reason, and content blocks from complete (non-streaming) API responses for both the Chat Completions and Responses API response formats, and maps HTTP error responses to categorized error results.

scope.in-scope: Parsing of complete (non-streaming) Chat Completions response bodies, parsing of complete Responses API response bodies, finish reason mapping for both response formats, usage extraction common to both formats, error detection from inline JSON error objects, and HTTP error categorization with JSON body refinement.
scope.boundary: Incremental streaming event handling for both Chat Completions and Responses API formats is covered by the OpenAI Chat Streaming and OpenAI Responses Streaming specifications. This specification covers only complete, buffered response parsing.

The string-to-enum mapping rules for finish reasons are documented in the Finish Reason Mapping specification. This specification documents when and how those mappings are applied during response parsing.

The retry eligibility classification of error categories is documented in the Provider Error Categorization specification. This specification documents the HTTP-to-category and code-refinement logic that feeds that system.
How requests are built and dispatched to the OpenAI APIs is out of scope.

field.name: model
field.note: exact model identifier string from the response; null if absent

field.name: finish_reason
field.note: internal finish reason (STOP, LENGTH, TOOL_USE, CONTENT_FILTER, ERROR, UNKNOWN)

field.name: usage.input_tokens
field.note: prompt token count; 0 if usage absent

field.name: usage.output_tokens
field.note: completion token count; 0 if usage absent

field.name: usage.total_tokens
field.note: total token count; 0 if usage absent

field.name: usage.thinking_tokens
field.note: reasoning token count from nested details; 0 if absent

field.name: content_blocks
field.note: ordered content blocks; null if none present

field.name: content_count
field.note: number of valid blocks in the array; 0 when content_blocks is null

field.name: text
field.note: the text content; never empty (Chat API) or absent only when text or refusal field missing (Responses API)

field.name: arguments
field.note: raw JSON string exactly as received; not parsed or validated

behavior.chat-response-parsing:
A Chat Completions response body is parsed as a JSON object. The parser checks for a top-level "error" object first; if present, parsing is aborted and a PROVIDER-category error is returned containing only the value of the error object's "message" field (defaulting to "Unknown error" if the field is absent or not a string).
When no top-level error object is present, the parser extracts:
- Model: the top-level "model" string field. If absent or not a string, the model field of the response is left null.
- Usage: from the top-level "usage" object. "prompt_tokens" maps to input tokens, "completion_tokens" to output tokens, "total_tokens" to total tokens. Reasoning tokens are read from the nested "completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens" field. Any absent or non-integer field is left as zero.
- Finish reason: from "choices[0].finish_reason". The Chat Completions finish reason string-to-enum table is applied. A null or absent value maps to UNKNOWN.
- Content: from "choices[0].message". A non-null, non-empty string "content" field produces one TEXT block. A "tool_calls" array produces one TOOL_CALL block per element. Text blocks are placed before tool call blocks in the output array. Each tool call block requires "id" (string), "function.name" (string), and "function.arguments" (string); a missing or non-string value for any of these fields causes the parser to return a PARSE error.
When the "choices" array is absent, empty, or "choices[0].message" is absent, the response is returned with null content and a count of zero, but the already-extracted model, usage, and finish reason fields are preserved. When neither "content" nor any "tool_calls" elements are present, the same null/zero outcome applies.
The "function.arguments" field in a Chat Completions tool call is a JSON string (not a JSON object). The parser stores this value verbatim without parsing, re-encoding, or validating it. Callers receive a JSON-encoded string that they must decode themselves.
A "content" field that is present but empty (zero-length string) is treated the same as a null or absent field. No TEXT block is produced for empty content.
behavior.responses-api-response-parsing:
A Responses API response body is parsed as a JSON object. The same top-level "error" object check applies: if present, a PROVIDER error is returned with the "message" field value.
When no error is present, the parser extracts:
- Model: the top-level "model" string field. If absent or not a string, the model field of the response is left null.
- Usage: identical field mapping as Chat Completions (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens, completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens).
- Finish reason: from the top-level "status" field, consulted together with "incomplete_details.reason" when status is "incomplete". The Responses API status-to-enum table is applied. A null or absent status maps to UNKNOWN.
- Content: from the top-level "output" array. Each item in the array has a "type" field. Items of unknown or absent type are silently skipped.
Items of type "message" have a "content" array. Each element of the content array has its own "type" field: - "output_text": a TEXT block is produced from the item's "text" string field. If the "text" field is absent or not a string, the item is silently skipped. - "refusal": a TEXT block is produced from the item's "refusal" string field. If the "refusal" field is absent or not a string, the item is silently skipped. Content items of any other type are silently skipped.
Items of type "function_call" produce one TOOL_CALL block. The identifier is taken from the "call_id" string field if present; otherwise from the "id" string field. If neither is present, the parser returns a PARSE error. "name" and "arguments" are required string fields; a missing or non-string value for either causes a PARSE error.
The parser pre-counts the expected number of content blocks from the output array before allocating, then fills them in a second pass. The final content count reflects only the blocks actually added; if any items are skipped during the second pass, the recorded count is lower than the pre-counted estimate.
For Responses API function call items, the "call_id" field takes precedence over the "id" field as the tool call identifier. If both are present, "call_id" is used and "id" is ignored. This differs from Chat Completions, where only the "id" field exists.
A "refusal" content item is surfaced as a TEXT block, not as a distinct block type. The refusal text is treated identically to regular output text from the caller's perspective.
Because skippable items (unknown content types, missing text/refusal fields) are only detected during the fill pass, the reported content count may be smaller than the number of slots allocated. Callers should rely on the reported count, not the allocation size.
behavior.usage-extraction:
Both Chat Completions and Responses API parsers apply the same usage extraction logic from a "usage" object:
- "prompt_tokens" (integer) → input token count - "completion_tokens" (integer) → output token count - "total_tokens" (integer) → total token count - "completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens" (integer, in a nested object) → thinking token count
Each field is independently optional. Missing, null, or non-integer values result in a count of zero for that field. The absence of the entire "usage" object results in all counts being zero.
behavior.error-response-parsing:
HTTP error responses (non-2xx) are processed separately from successful response bodies. The HTTP status code is mapped to an initial error category:
- 400 → INVALID_ARG - 401, 403 → AUTH - 404 → NOT_FOUND - 429 → RATE_LIMIT - 500, 502, 503 → SERVER - All other codes → UNKNOWN
The response body is then parsed as JSON to attempt category refinement and message extraction. If the body cannot be parsed as valid JSON or the root is not an object, the parser silently falls back to the HTTP-derived category with a message of "HTTP {status}".
When a valid JSON error object is found at the "error" key, the "type", "code", and "message" string fields are extracted. Category refinement proceeds in this order:
1. Content filter detection: if the "type" or "code" field contains the substring "content_filter" anywhere, the category is overridden to CONTENT_FILTER, regardless of the HTTP status. This check takes priority over all other refinements.
2. Code-based refinement (applied only when content filter was not detected): if "code" is one of the following known values, the category is overridden: - "invalid_api_key" or "invalid_org" → AUTH - "rate_limit_exceeded" or "quota_exceeded" → RATE_LIMIT - "model_not_found" → NOT_FOUND
The error message is formatted from the extracted fields according to which are present: - type, code, and message all present: "{type} ({code}): {message}" - type and message present, code absent: "{type}: {message}" - message present, type absent: message string used directly - type present, message absent: type string used directly - none present: "HTTP {status}"
Content filter detection uses substring matching, not exact equality. Any "type" or "code" value that contains "content_filter" anywhere within it triggers the CONTENT_FILTER override. This means a value like "request_content_filter_error" would match.
When the error response body cannot be parsed as JSON, the parser does not surface a parse error to the caller. It returns success with the HTTP-derived category and a plain "HTTP {status}" message string. Parse failures are not propagated.
The success-path parsers (Chat Completions and Responses API) also check for a top-level "error" object in otherwise valid JSON. This path is distinct from the HTTP error path: it fires when the HTTP status is nominally successful but the body contains an error object, and it returns a PROVIDER-category error (not a categorized HTTP error) with only the "message" field. The type and code fields are not consulted on this path.
